items.title: A FRONT-ROW SEAT TO THE RENAISSANCE: WHY KL FAMILY STAYS ARE CHANGING
items.content:
We all know the struggle of the typical "city stay" with the family. 

It starts with logistics: how far is the hotel from the LRT? Is there a place nearby where they can run around that isn’t a busy road? By the time you’ve ticked off the convenience boxes, you often find yourself sacrificing the feeling of an actual getaway.

But as we look toward 2026 in Kuala Lumpur, there’s one landmark that has quietly solved that riddle, recently being honoured as Asia's Best Family City Hotel

Photo credit:Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

The Renaissance Kuala Lumpur Hotel & Convention Centre has managed to do something rare in downtown KL by creating a space that portrays itself not just as a hotel, but as a dual-branded urban resort where families can set up their "home base" right in the heart of the action.
                                                                                 Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

The Skyline View is Their Storyteller

When we talk about an "unforgettable stay" in 2026, the experience starts before you even unpack. For a child, seeing the city from the floor-to-ceiling windows of their room isn't just a view; it’s a fairy tale of scale.

The unique perspective here places you in a living conversation between KL’s iconic past and its soaring future. Waking up to the KL Tower on one side and the stunning geometry of the brand-new Merdeka 118 on the other frames their entire stay. 

In 2026, those specific rooms offer the best front-row seat to the changing city they will one day read about in history books.

                                                                          Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

13,000 Square Feet of "Unforgettable"

The real portrait of a Renaissance family is painted on Level 4. You wouldn’t expect to find Paradise Trails tucked between skyscrapers—a 13,000-square-foot outdoor adventure zone dedicated entirely to play.

Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

Here, "unforgettable" looks like this:

  • The Canopy Walk: A chance to walk among the "tops" of the palms, right above the city.

Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur


  • The Climbing Wall: Safe, outdoor challenges designed to help them find their confidence.

Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

  • The Massive Pool: One of the largest outdoor pools in the city, complete with water slides that turn a morning dip into a mini-theme park adventure.

Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

While the little ones explore their private sanctuary, parents can actually relax at the Mandara Spa or enjoy a "Bar Ritual" at R Bar, knowing their children are making memories just a few steps away.



Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur


Festive Traditions, Reinvented for 2026

If you are planning your 2026 around the holidays, the Renaissance is offering a way to drop the holiday stress and keep the magic.

When you book your stay on Christmas Eve or New Year’s Eve, the portrait changes from adventure to celebration. 

It starts with the exclusive "wake up to the skyline" morning, followed by an Evolution breakfast where the authentic Italian coastal flavours meet the local festive spirit. 

For the Eve of the celebration, the family-friendly Holiday Buffet ensures that everyone—from the most adventurous eater to the pickiest toddler—finds a feast they love.

Photo credit: Renaissance Kuala Lumpur

In 2026...

A family staying here isn't just about finding a room near the Twin Towers.

It’s about securing a place where your family can breathe, play, and watch the future rise right outside your window.

So, what are you waiting for? Book your stay now!

items.title: THE GLOW UP RAYA AT SUNGEI WANG PLAZA
items.content:
This Hari Raya season, Sungei Wang Plaza invites shoppers to celebrate with “The Glow Up Raya”, a vibrant festive campaign happening from 28 February to 29 March 2026. Held in support of Tourism Malaysia and the Visit Malaysia 2026, the celebration brings together culture, music, festive shopping and meaningful community initiatives.

Photo credit: Sungei Wang Plaza

A Kampung-Inspired Festive Experience

Visitors will be welcomed by a charming kampung-inspired festive décor at the mall’s Centre Court and Main Entrance. Featuring traditional village house elements, batik-inspired decorations and warm festive lighting, the installation blends Malaysian heritage with the vibrant energy of Kuala Lumpur, creating a perfect setting for festive photos and family moments.

Photo credit: Sungei Wang Plaza

Glow Up Raya Musical Showcase

The highlight of the campaign takes place on 29 March 2026 with the Glow Up Raya Musical Showcase, hosted by Sha Sha from ERA FM.

Photo credit: Sungei Wang Plaza


The showcase features performances by popular Malaysian artistes including:

  • MimiFly
  • Aufa Hanie
  • Zubir Khan
  • Ammar Haikal

Visitors can expect an exciting afternoon filled with music, festive energy and memorable Raya moments.


Festive Activities & Workshops


Throughout the campaign, visitors can enjoy various activities including cultural dance performances, the Festival Warisan Nusantara Fashion Show, live music showcases, and the Ketupat Mascot Walkabout.

Photo credit: Sungei Wang Plaza

Families can also join the Raya Miniature Workshop by MinNature Malaysia, held at JUMPA, where participants can create their own festive miniature scenes.


Raya Bazaar & Rewards


The Glow Up Raya Bazaar at Centre Court offers a lively marketplace filled with festive treats, Raya essentials and unique finds from local vendors.

Photo credit: Sungei Wang Plaza

Shoppers can also enjoy special redemptions, including Raya packets, tiffins and an exclusive cookie jar for members.


Giving Back This Raya


Through the Raya Give and Glow initiative, visitors can donate pre-loved clothing to support refugee communities in collaboration with United Arakan Institute Malaysia. The mall will also host a special Berbuka Puasa session with Pusat Gelendangan Anjung Kembara, spreading the spirit of generosity during the holy month of Ramadan.


Celebrate the joy of Raya with culture, entertainment and community spirit at Sungei Wang Plaza this festive season.


#TheGlowUpRaya #RayaatSungeiWangPlaza #SungeiWangPlaza

items.title: MAKE THIS SCHOOL HOLIDAY UNFORGETTABLE AT BOMB BATTLE!
items.content:
Looking for the ultimate fun this school holiday? Bomb Battle is Malaysia’s go-to action playground with multiple outlets nationwide!


 Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

Its signature Paint Bomb game at Berjaya Times Square immerses players in a high-tech, life-like battlefield where teamwork, strategy, and fast reflexes are the key to victory. 

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

Not only that, Bomb Battle also features Escape Room experiences, each with unique themes that challenge your mind and ignite imagination. Kids learn to think creatively while having endless fun!

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle


Step into the viral Floor is Lava game, a childhood classic reimagined for all ages! The gameplay is simple yet exciting, collecting points by stepping on the green tiles while avoiding the red “lava” tiles. Jump, climb, and navigate your way to a high score for thrilling fun that’s perfect for kids and adults alike. You can enjoy this game at two of our outlets: Atria Shopping Gallery and The Curve.

 

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

Then, bring out your competitive side at the Versus Arena, where friends become rivals and families battle it out! Compete across four mini-games full of action, laughs, and hilarious fails. It’s loud, fast, and utterly FUN. 

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

Looking for a location that’s closer to home? Our Bomb Battle at The Curve offers the same signature fun but with new challenges! Paint Bomb battles are unique to each outlet, and the Versus Arena includes exciting features like a giant ball pit for kids to explore and conquer.

 

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle


Want a totally different, next-level experience? Get ready, because Bomb Battle flagship outlet at Sunway Pyramid delivers pure WOW! Featuring the Gel Blaster Nexus, the first of its kind in Asia and imported from the USA, This smart blaster arena tracks your score on a leaderboard, giving you the thrill of paintball with less pain, less mess, and more high-tech fun. Excitement, strategy, and action await, with prices starting from just RM45!

  

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

 

Step into a life-sized, arcade-style escape room and tackle 13 quirky missions! Solve puzzles, complete challenges, and collect physical coins to redeem real prizes. Don’t miss the ultimate finale, Bike Battle, where you wager your coins and the winner takes it all!

 

Photo Credit: Bomb Battle

 

Feeling competitive? Try Malaysia’s first-ever axe-throwing arena at Sunway Pyramid! Learn from our pro marshals, challenge your friends, and see who hits the bullseye. Fun, thrilling, and perfect for stress relief. For ages 18+ only.

This school holiday, make memories your kids will never forget! Come to Bomb Battle and enjoy immersive games, mind-challenging puzzles, and adrenaline-pumping arenas that bring out the inner champion in everyone! Visit their website or check out their Instagram now to grab the deal!

items.title: CHIC & UNIQUE SOUVENIRS: WELCOME TO TREASURE TROVE!
items.content:
Treasure Trove at Kuala Lumpur Central Walk feels like stumbling upon a tiny treasure box of Malaysian creativity, where every shelf is packed with colourful finds that celebrate local culture, craft, and design in a way that feels fun, meaningful, and actually worth bringing home instead of the usual generic souvenir-shop plastic trinkets.

Treasure Trove was created with the mission of giving Malaysian small businesses and artists the visibility they deserve, turning the boutique into a curated platform where passionate homegrown creators can showcase their work while visitors get to discover the richness of Malaysia’s arts, culture, and craftsmanship all in one place.

Photo Credit: Treasure Trove 

Being located right in the heart of Kuala Lumpur Central Walk gives the store a lively city-core atmosphere, with locals popping in during lunch breaks, office workers browsing after work, and travellers wandering in curious to see what unique Malaysian treasures they can find, creating a space that feels vibrant yet welcoming.

The overall vibe and aesthetic can best be described as modern Malaysian, where heritage motifs, traditional symbols, and cultural references are reimagined into contemporary lifestyle items that feel stylish, practical, and colourful while still staying deeply connected to Malaysian roots.

Photo Credit: Treasure Trove

Supporting local artisans is truly the heart of Treasure Trove, because every product in the store represents a real creator whose work carries their personal inspiration, cultural background, and craftsmanship, making each piece feel more like a story than just another item on a shelf.

If you only have a few minutes to browse but still want something memorable to bring home, the store’s signature picks include handcrafted ceramic mugs featuring Malaysian icons like the Bunga Raya, Rafflesia, hornbills, and traditional wau kites, handmade pop-up cards inspired by nostalgic local scenes and traditional kuih, and batik-inspired accessories such as keycaps or even pet bibs that cleverly mix heritage design with modern creativity.

What makes the shopping experience even more special is that the team actively shares the stories behind the makers, so customers often learn about the artists’ inspiration, creative process, and personal journeys through friendly conversations in-store, thoughtful product descriptions, and social media features that highlight the people behind each creation.

KL locals who visit Treasure Trove often gravitate toward nostalgic everyday items that remind them of familiar Malaysian moments, including ceramic mugs, decorative figurines, postcards, handmade greeting cards, magnets, and charming stationery featuring local slang, cultural references, and playful nods to Malaysian food and festivities.

When it comes to gift shopping, Treasure Trove stands out because every item carries a deeper meaning, with thoughtfully curated pieces that represent Malaysian creativity and craftsmanship, making the gifts feel far more personal and heartfelt than something mass-produced.

Photo Credit: Treasure Trove

Behind the scenes, the store also maintains strong relationships with its network of artists and creators, regularly collaborating with them, sharing feedback, and supporting their work through in-store features and promotional activities so the creative community can continue to grow together.

If you are planning to visit, Treasure Trove is open Monday to Saturday from 10am to 7pm, with lunchtime being the busiest period, so those who want a more relaxed browsing experience will probably enjoy coming right when the shop opens at 10am or later in the afternoon after 2pm.

Looking ahead, the team is also working on new ideas and collaborations for 2026, which means visitors can expect even more artisanal products and creative collections that showcase Malaysia’s diverse cultural influences and evolving creative scene.

Ultimately, the feeling Treasure Trove hopes customers take home with them is nostalgia, because every mug, card, or handcrafted piece is meant to become a warm reminder of Malaysia, its culture, and the memories and love people create while exploring Kuala Lumpur.

LOCATION: Treasure Trove 百宝箱 @ Kuala Lumpur Central Walk (KLCW), Kuala Lumpur City Centre, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
OPERATING HOURS: 10:00 AM - 7:00 PM (Monday - Saturday) | Closed (Sunday) WEBSITE / ONLINE: https://www.instagram.com/treasuretrove_klcw

items.title: FARM IN THE CITY: KAMPUNG VIBES & FURRY SURPRISES
items.content:
If you’ve been craving something wholesome in Kuala Lumpur that isn’t another mall
or cafe, Farm in The City is the kind of place that resets your mood the second you step in.

It’s an award-winning Malaysian village-themed petting zoo that feels like a mini
kampung world tucked into the city, where nature and community vibes actually
show up in real life.

The best part is you’re not just “looking at animals,” you’re properly meeting them, the
kind of visit where a colourful bird might land on your shoulder and you suddenly feel
like you’re in a fairytale.

Photo Credit: Farm In The City

And yes, the Sunny Oasis Station is now home to capybaras , those famously
relaxed creatures that seem permanently unbothered by life and unbelievably
cute in person.

If you’re bringing kids along, the Junior Farm Ranger program is genuinely a flex
because they get hands-on animal care experience like grooming and feeding instead of
just walking around bored.

It’s also one of those rare attractions where the “included in your ticket” line actually
means something, because the animal interactions are a real highlight and not a hidden
extra charge situation.

You’ll want to time your visit around their Daily Feeding and Interaction Schedule
so you don’t miss the fun moments, from playful otters to the raccoon hugging
interaction that people talk about after like it’s a core memory.

What makes this spot tourist-friendly is how easy it is to reach now, because you can
literally MRT your way there and skip the traffic pain entirely.

It’s the kind of day trip that works whether you’re visiting KL for the first time, travelling
with family, or just want a break that feels outdoorsy without needing a full road trip plan.

If you’re building a KL itinerary, this is a clean “add it in” option because it’s cute,
different, interactive, and honestly just refreshing compared to the usual city loop.

Before you go: Online tickets must be purchased at least 24 hours before your visit, so plan it like a proper day out instead of last-minute winging it.

How to get there (MRT shortcut): Take the MRT Putrajaya Line to Putra Permai Station (PY37), then it’s about a 5-minute walk to the farm.

Operating hours: Weekdays: 10:00am - 6:00pm, weekends/public holidays/school holidays:  9:30am - 6:00pm, and they’re closed every Tuesday (unless it’s a public or school holiday).

Address: Lot 40187-40188, Jalan Prima Tropika Barat 1, Pusat Bandar Putra Permai,
43300 Seri Kembangan, Selangor.

Contact: 03-8941 3099 / WhatsApp 010-762 7612.

Website: https://farminthecity.my/

items.title: THE YELLOW LANDMARK OF CHINATOWN: WHY BUNN CHOON IS A MUST-VISIT AT KWAI CHAI HONG
items.content:
If you find yourself wandering through the hidden murals and pre-war charm of Kwai Chai Hong, your journey through the Straits of Malacca influence in KL isn't truly complete until you step into the vibrant yellow building on the corner.

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

Bunn Choon 1893 isn’t just a bakery; it is a living piece of Kuala Lumpur’s history.

Standing as a golden landmark at the edge of the heritage trail, this iconic shop is the ultimate "must-visit" destination.

To visit Kwai Chai Hong and miss Bunn Choon is to miss the very soul of Chinatown.

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

Step Into Authentic Nostalgia

While the murals outside tell the stories of the past, Bunn Choon allows you to taste it. As you move from the bustling streets into the heritage-style interior, the atmosphere shifts.

From the vintage memorabilia to the traditional wooden plaque hanging proudly in the dining area, every corner of this restaurant breathes authentic nostalgia.

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

The Gold Standard of Specialty Bakes

You cannot visit Kwai Chai Hong without indulging in the legendary bakes that have defined this brand for over 130 years.

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

Bunn Choon has earned its reputation as a trusted specialty bakehouse, famous for:

  • Signature Egg Tarts: Renowned for their impossibly flaky crust and silky custard.

  • Traditional Heritage Treats: Handcrafted recipes that have remained unchanged since 1893.

  • Century-Old Legacy: Every bite is a testament to the craftsmanship that has survived generations.

The Essential Kwai Chai Hong Pitstop

Whether you are a photography enthusiast capturing the teal windows against the yellow facade, or a foodie hunting for KL’s best tarts, Bunn Choon is your essential one-stop destination. It is the perfect place to soak in the old-world charm and experience the heritage of the area in its most delicious form.

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

Plan Your Heritage Visit

The best way to experience Bunn Choon 1893 is to catch them while the tarts are fresh out of the oven! To ensure you don't miss out on your favourite specialty bakes, do take note of their 

Operating Hours:

  • Tuesday to Sunday: 7:30 AM – 3:30 PM

  • Monday: Closed (The team is resting to prep for the week ahead)

Photo credit: Bunn Choon via Instagram

Stay Connected with the Legacy 

Want to see what’s baking or check for seasonal specialties before you head over? You can find more details on their official website at bunnchoon1893.my.

For your daily dose of flaky pastry shots and heritage vibes, follow their journey on Instagram at @bunnchoon1893 and Facebook at @bunnchoon1893.

items.title: CAMP5: CLIMBING FOR EVERYONE, ADVENTURE FOR ALL
items.content:
From families to adventure seekers, Camp5’s goal is simple: get everyone climbing

Camp5 is synonymous with indoor climbing in Malaysia. With six gyms nationwide — five in Greater Kuala Lumpur and one in Johor Bahru — Camp5 has helped shape the country’s modern climbing culture. 

Walk into any outlet and you’ll instantly feel inspired. 

Beginners tackle their first wall, kids scramble up colourful holds, and experienced climbers project tough routes overhead.

Photo credit: Camp5

Each location has its own personality, but all share the same foundations — accessibility, safety, professional route setting, and a genuinely welcoming atmosphere.

No experience? Not necessary. 

Camp5 offers a full range of courses, from Bouldering to Lead Climbing and even Outdoor Lead Climbing. There’s a pathway for every comfort level.

Photo credit: Camp5

For families, the Family Friendly Courses are a fantastic introduction. 

You’ll learn safety basics and simple techniques together, then stay on to climb at your own pace. 

No pressure, no intimidation — just encouragement.

From families to adventure seekers and even professional athletes, climbing truly is for everyone.


CAMP5 ECO CITY: SOUTHEAST ASIA’S LARGEST BOULDERING GYM

Photo credit: Camp5

Camp5 Eco City is a true boulderer’s playground. 

Spanning 3,000sqm, it features nearly 1,000sqm of dedicated bouldering terrain, alongside 33 top-rope lines, autobelays, and two lead climbing lanes. 

Tension and Moon boards are available for those focused on building strength and refining technique.

Routes are reset regularly, so there’s always something new to challenge yourself with  whether you’re dropping in for a casual session or committing to serious training.

Families are well catered for too, with a dedicated kids’ area designed to help young climbers build confidence in a safe and structured environment. 

Add a barista café serving coffee and healthy meals, plus showers and locker rooms, and it’s easy to see why many climbers spend hours here.

Eco City isn’t just a gym — it’s a second home!

Photo credit: Camp5


CAMP5 KL EAST: FAMILY ADVENTURE MEETS FULL-SERVICE CLIMBING

Located at KL East Mall, just minutes from the iconic Batu Caves crags, Camp5 KL East blends indoor comfort with outdoor inspiration.

Split across two floors, Level G3 houses the Play Climb wall and gear shop. Upstairs — or via direct access from Carpark L1 — you’ll find the main climbing hall.

Here, climbers can enjoy bouldering, 12-metre top-rope routes with autobelays, and stunning 15-metre lead climbing. 

The fully air-conditioned space also offers training walls, functional fitness facilities, hot showers, locker rooms, and the Eat Well Café — everything you’d expect from a modern climbing gym.

Photo credit: Camp5

Play Climb: Perfect for Young Adventurers

What truly sets KL East apart is Play Climb. 

It’s a safe, structured and exciting way for little adventurers to discover climbing — and parents can join in too.

Designed especially for younger climbers, this colourful space turns climbing into a fun, confidence-building experience. 

Hour-long guided sessions run throughout the day, making it easy to plan a visit.

Children must be at least 4 years old and weigh a minimum of 10kg. 

The fee includes a harness and helmet, and kids can wear their own sport shoes or trainers (no flip-flops or slippers).

Photo credit: Camp5


Photo credit: Camp5


CAMP5 UTROPOLIS: A COMMUNITY FAVOURITE

Located in Glenmarie, Shah Alam, Camp5 Utropolis is popular with students and seasoned climbers alike.

Known for its spacious layout and strong bouldering focus, it offers everything from beginner-friendly routes to dynamic, parkour-style moves.

Utropolis balances performance and community beautifully. 

It remains welcoming to first-timers and families, with introductory courses and supportive staff ready to guide new climbers.

Photo credit: Camp5


CAMP5 1 UTAMA: SOUTHEAST ASIA’S TALLEST LEAD WALL

Camp5’s beloved flagship at 1 Utama recently celebrated 20 years of operation. 

Located inside one of Malaysia’s largest shopping malls, it makes climbing incredibly accessible — perfect for an after-work session or a weekend activity.

The gym offers a full climbing experience, including bouldering, roped climbing, and training facilities. 

Daily Try Climbing sessions make it easy for beginners to get started, while dedicated kids’ programmes cater to young climbers from as young as one year old.

Photo credit: Camp5

The Katak Club parent-and-child sessions are designed to nurture a lifelong love of climbing. 

After just a few sessions, many parents feel confident enough to bring their children climbing independently. 

Photo credit: Camp5
Photo credit: Camp5

A fun bonus: kids under eight climb free with a checked-in guardian at all Camp5 facilities.

And for those ready to dream big, Camp5 1 Utama houses the tallest indoor lead wall in Southeast Asia — a towering feature that inspires climbers to quite literally reach new heights.

Photo credit: Camp5


BEAST PARK AT CAMP5 JUMPA: FITNESS MEETS ADVENTURE

In the heart of Bukit Bintang, on the third floor of Jumpa at Sungei Wang Plaza, nestled within the walls of a full scale climbing gym in Camp5 Jumpa, you’lll also find Beast Park — a high-energy ninja course created by the Camp5 team.

Photo credit: Camp5

With the All-Access Beast Park Pass, visitors get 90 minutes to explore Ninja-style obstacle courses, interactive E-walls, the Fun Cave, Lil’ Beast zones, and the elevated Beast Net. 

It’s playful, active, and just challenging enough to keep things exciting.

Weekday passes are more budget-friendly, while weekends and public holidays are slightly higher. 

For even more action, the Combo Adventure + Climbing pass pairs Beast Park access with a guided climbing session.

Additional programs include Katak Club sessions, corporate bookings, birthday parties, and a full range of climbing courses.

Photo credit: Camp5


MORE THAN JUST CLIMBING

Across its six locations, Camp5 has built more than climbing gyms — it has built a thriving community. 

It’s where beginners gain confidence, families bond over shared challenges, and experienced climbers continue pushing their limits.

Whether you’re starting a new hobby, planning a family day out, or chasing your next fitness goal, Camp5 Climbing Gym might just become your new favourite place to hang out — and climb a little higher.

Photo credit: Camp5


Ready to start your adventure?

With six locations across Greater Kuala Lumpur and Johor Bahru, there is a Camp5 wall waiting for you. 

For specific addresses, contact details, and Operating Hours for each outlet, please visit the official website or follow Camp5 on social media for daily updates.

Website: www.camp5.com

items.title: SEE MALAYSIA CLEARLY: THE VISTA EYE CARNIVAL RETURNS THIS MARCH
items.content:
Clear vision is a vital part of experiencing life and the beauty of our surroundings. This coming March, VISTA Eye Specialist is hosting its nationwide "Malaysia Truly Eye-sia" Eye Carnival, offering a comprehensive range of screenings and corrective procedure offers across two consecutive weekends.

Photo credit: VISTA Eye Specialist

The carnival is scheduled to take place from 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM on the following dates and locations:

  • 6 - 8 March 2026: Klang, Cheras, Kepong, Ipoh Falim, Tanjung Tokong, Bukit Mertajam, and JB Sutera Utama.

  • 13 - 15 March 2026: PJ The Curve, Puchong, Kajang, Ipoh Bond Square, Air Itam (Penang), and JB Mount Austin.

Specialised Corrective Offers

For those exploring permanent vision correction, the event highlights significant offers on primary procedures. 

LASIK surgery is available starting from RM1,998 per eye, while Cataract surgery starts from RM3,388 per eye. 

To assist with accessibility, the specialist is also offering 0% interest installment plans for up to 24 months.

Photo credit: VISTA Eye Specialist

Eye Disease Screening

A major focus of the carnival is preventative care through dedicated screening tracks for different age groups:

  • For Adults: Screenings cover critical conditions such as Cataracts, Glaucoma, and Dry Eye.

  • For Kids (Aged 5 and Above): Assessments include checks for Squint Eye, Astigmatism, and Shortsightedness.

Attendees can also benefit from specialist consultations and suitability tests for CLEAR Pro/LASIK to determine the best course of action for their specific ocular needs.

Registration Details
Social Media: Instagram @vista.eye | Facebook @vista.eye

Admission to the Eye Carnival is free for all visitors. However, interested participants are encouraged to register in advance to secure their screening slots and consultation times.

Photo credit: VISTA Eye Specialist

Whether you’re looking to upgrade your vision for the year ahead or want a professional check-up for the family, this nationwide event provides a convenient, one-stop platform for all things eye care. 


The campaign also features collaborations with selected health and wellness partners, where visitors can enjoy complimentary experiences such as chiropractic assessments, dental screenings, aromatherapy, and more—creating a holistic health initiative that integrates vision care with overall well-being.

Operating Hours: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM (Carnival Days)

Locations: Various (refer to schedule)

items.title: WALLET-FRIENDLY WINGS: HOW SHOPLAH AND NEW PRICING RULES ARE TRANSFORMING KLIA
items.content:
Ever felt that distinct pinch in your wallet the moment you stepped into an airport terminal?

We have all been there, staring at a bottle of water or a simple meal that somehow costs twice as much as it does in the city.

With Visit Malaysia 2026 (VM2026) fast approaching, the government is stepping in to make sure your airport experience doesn't leave a sour taste in your mouth.

Photo credit: Asean Food Travel

The Ministry of Tourism, Arts and Culture (MOTAC) has officially urged Malaysia Airports Holdings Berhad (MAHB) to keep a closer eye on food and retail pricing.

This move comes after a wave of complaints regarding high prices at our main gateways, specifically KLIA Terminal 1 and KLIA Terminal 2.

The goal is to ensure that the first and last impression of Malaysia is one of hospitality, not overpricing, effectively reducing travel fatigue.

To balance the scales, MAHB has officially activated a new, enhanced phase of ShopLAH to make airport shopping more rewarding for the global traveller.

While ShopLAH originally debuted back in 2019, this 10 February 2026 relaunch marks its evolution into an "always-on" retail brand specifically for the VM2026 cycle.

Whether you are flying in from across the region or hopping between continents, the focus is now on fair value and high-quality local engagement.

Pro-Tip: The "Food Garden" Secret

For the most "local" prices in KLIA Terminal 1, skip the departure hall chains and head to the Food Garden on Level 2 (Main Terminal Building).

Recently revamped, this food court is the primary dining spot for airport staff, meaning you’ll find authentic Nasi Kandar and Chicken Rice at prices much closer to downtown KL.

items.title: SWEET DEALS AND GOLDEN BLESSINGS: WHY MANDARIN ORANGES ARE CHEAPER THIS CNY 2026
items.content:
If you’ve been walking through the aisles of Village Grocer or your local Bukit Bintang fruit stalls lately, you might have noticed something refreshing and it isn’t just the citrus scent.

Photo credit: The Star

Mandarin orange prices have seen a significant drop across Malaysia this year, just in time for the Year of the Horse celebrations starting 17 February 2026.

While initial reports centred on Melaka, we’ve verified that this "citrus surplus" is nationwide, including right here in Kuala Lumpur and the Klang Valley.

The festive price cut is largely thanks to a strengthening Malaysian Ringgit, which hit a seven-year high against the US dollar recently, and a massive bumper harvest from orchards in China.

But as any savvy local knows, we don’t just buy these oranges because they are a bargain; we buy them because they are literal "gold."

In Cantonese, the word for mandarin orange is kam, which sounds exactly like the word for "gold."

This linguistic coincidence is why the act of gifting oranges, known as song gam, is essentially like wishing someone a year filled with actual riches.

It is also why you’ll always see them served in pairs; in Chinese culture, good things come in twos, and a single orange is considered a bit "lonely" for a house full of blessings.

Beyond the wealth factor, the round, vibrant shape of the orange is said to resemble the sun, aligning with the yang (positive) principle to bring abundance and happiness into the home.

Whether you are stuffing them into a gift basket or placing them on your office desk, these fruits are the ultimate "low-cost, high-reward" way to manifest a prosperous year.

So, while the prices are down, it’s the perfect time to stock up, because in 2026, we’re all about sharing the "gold" without breaking the bank.

Just remember to keep an eye on the stems; a fresh green leaf still attached is the ultimate sign that your "gold" is as fresh as it gets.

2026 CNY Citrus Guide

  • The Price Trend: Prices are down approximately 10% to 30% nationwide compared to last year.
  • Trending Varieties: Look for the "Yellow Beauty" (sweet and firm) or the "Jelly Mandarin" (seedless and fiber-free), which are the top premium picks in KL malls this season.
  • Estimated Costs: Value boxes of Yongchun Lokam are retailing between RM14 – RM23, while premium gift sets (like Yellow Beauty) are ranging from RM35 – RM48.

items.title: FROM THE PENANG HILL FIRE TO PORTUGUESE ROOTS: WHY DO WE ACTUALLY SAY ‘BOMBA’?
items.content:
If you saw the smoke rising over Jalan Kebun Bunga yesterday, you weren't alone in feeling a bit of a heart-flutter for our beloved Penang Botanic Gardens.

Photo credit: The Star

Initial reports had everyone worried that the gardens themselves were ablaze, but the Penang Hill Corporation (PHC) has since clarified that the five-hectare fire was actually at KM1.9 along the Penang Hill jeep track.

Thanks to the rapid response from the Penang Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) and the Air Itam Bukit Bendera Volunteer Fire Brigade, the fire was brought under control before it could do any major damage to our historic trees.

It’s during these intense moments, when the heat is on and the sirens are wailing, that we realise just how lucky we are to have the Bomba on speed dial.

Photo credit: Jabatan Bomba & Penyelamat Malaysia (Fire & Rescue Department of Malaysia)

But here’s a quick "pro-tip" for your next trivia night: have you ever wondered why we actually call them the "Bomba"?

You might have heard the popular theory that it's an acronym for Badan Operasi Mencegah Bencana Alam.

While that sounds incredibly official, Melaka Fire and Rescue director Mohd Zaidi Ma'at recently set the record straight: the term actually has Portuguese roots.

The word is derived from the Portuguese bombeiros, which literally means "firefighters."

According to historical records, the term entered local usage during the Portuguese era in Melaka and was later adopted officially in 1961.

Many people assume it has something to do with "bombs" or explosions, but it actually refers back to the Portuguese word bomba, meaning "pump": the essential tool for moving water to fight fires.

While other neighbours might use direct translations like "api dan penyelamat," Malaysia stuck with this unique linguistic souvenir from our colonial past in the Straits of Malacca.

The department itself has evolved through many names since it started as a Volunteer Fire Brigade in 1883.

Today, the team is even pushing a new aspiration called "Kita Bomba," focusing on values like bravery, integrity, and shared identity.

So, the next time you see those iconic red trucks racing toward Penang Hill or through the streets of Kuala Lumpur, you’ll know you’re looking at a legacy that's centuries in the making.

With the current dry season making things a bit crispy, let’s do our part to keep our green spaces safe because even the best "pumpsters" in the world prefer a quiet shift.

Quick Summary:

  • The News: A 5-hectare bushfire was controlled at KM1.9 along the Penang Hill jeep track on 4 February 2026.
  • The Myth: It is not an acronym for Badan Operasi Mencegah Bencana Alam, despite what many believe.
  • The Origin: "Bomba" comes from the Portuguese bombeiros (firefighters) and bomba (pump).
  • Historical Fact: The term was officially adopted in 1961, though the department’s roots go back to 1883.
  • Pro-Tip: During this dry spell, avoid any open burning near forest reserves like Penang Hill to prevent accidental reignition!

items.title: WAVE GOODBYE TO AIRPORT QUEUES: QR CODES ARE COMING!
items.content:
If you’ve ever felt that post-flight travel fatigue hit a wall while standing in a never-ending immigration line, I have some brilliant news for you.

Kuala Lumpur is about to get a lot more high-tech, and your smartphone is officially becoming your best travel buddy.

Photo credit: Bernama

Starting 1 March 2026, Malaysia Airports (MAHB) is rolling out QR code clearance across our major international gateways to make your arrival smoother than ever.

This is all part of the massive National Integrated Immigration System (MyNIISe), a digital upgrade designed to handle the millions of visitors we’re expecting for Visit Malaysia 2026 (VM2026).

The days of fumbling for your passport at the eGate are numbered; soon, you’ll just scan a code on your phone and breeze right through.

We’ve already seen this tech working wonders at the Johor-Singapore Causeway, and now it’s finally landing at KLIA Terminal 1 and Terminal 2.

The Home Ministry is installing 46 new pedestrian eGates across five airports to ensure a seamless transit experience, moving you from the plane to a taxi in record time.

Because a seamless transit only works if the hardware is actually there, here is exactly how the new eGate rollout looks across the country:

In our home base at KLIA Terminal 1, they are installing 18 units, split evenly between departures and arrivals to keep the flow moving.

Over at KLIA Terminal 2, you’ll see nine departure units ready to go, followed by an additional 10 arrival units coming in April 2026.

If you’re flying into the Pearl of the Orient, Bayan Lepas International Airport in Penang is getting five departure units and six arrival units.

And for our friends in East Malaysia, both Kota Kinabalu International Airport and Kuching International Airport are getting four units each to speed up your entry.

It’s a massive logistical upgrade for Malaysia Airports (MAHB), ensuring that no matter where you land, you aren’t stuck in a bottleneck.

For the tech-curious, this isn't just a simple scanner; it's a fully integrated ecosystem that syncs your travel data automatically to reduce repetitive checks.

Think of it as a transit sanctuary for your stress levels: less paperwork, more holiday.

Visit Malaysia 2026 is all about showing the world our hospitality, and that starts the second you step off the plane.

So, make sure your phone is charged when you land, because the future of Malaysian travel is officially just a scan away.

Photo credit: The Straits Times

Quick Summary:

  • The Date: QR code clearance begins 1 March 2026.
  • The Locations: KLIA (T1 & T2), Penang (Bayan Lepas), Kota Kinabalu, and Kuching International Airports.
  • The Rollout: 46 new eGates in total, with KLIA Terminal 2 getting an extra boost of 10 arrival units in April 2026.
  • The Tech: Driven by the MyNIISe app, replacing legacy systems for faster checkpoint clearance.
  • Pro-Tip: Download the MyNIISe app before you fly and register your profile early to avoid any "first-time" setup delays at the gate!

items.title: MORE THAN A BACKDROP: STEP INSIDE THE ICONIC BANGUNAN SULTAN ABDUL SAMAD
items.content:
If you’ve ever stood at Dataran Merdeka, you’ve definitely looked up at the Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad and wondered what was behind those heavy wooden doors.

Photo credit: The Star

For over a century, those doors remained largely closed to the public, marking a boundary between the "official" world and the rest of us.

Originally built in the late 1890s to house the British colonial administration, this building was designed to be the ultimate symbol of power and governance in the Straits of Malacca.

Later, it served as the heartbeat of the Malaysian legal system, housing the High Court and the Supreme Court, where some of the nation’s most pivotal legal battles were fought.

Photo credit: The Star

But as of 2 February 2026, the Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad is no longer just a distant backdrop for your holiday photos.

It has officially transitioned from a restricted government office into a living heritage museum for the people.

You might see the tagline "Six Buildings. Six Roles. One National Story" during your visit, and it’s the key to understanding this massive site.

The BSAS Complex is actually part of a massive RM600 million Warisan KL initiative by Khazanah Nasional to rejuvenate eight of KL's most historic landmarks.

This includes the Sultan Abdul Samad Building, the Pejabat Pos Lama, the former High Court, Panggung Bandaraya, the old FMS Survey Office, and the National Textile Museum.

As this is a Phase 1 opening, you’ll be exploring Block 1 (the main building), while restoration continues on the other blocks to bring the full "National Story" to life in the coming years.

Photo credit: The Star

But there is so much more than just history here; it’s a full lifestyle destination.

Visitors can now enjoy immersive exhibition spaces, a one-stop visitor centre, and even experiential retail and craft-making spaces to get hands-on with local culture.

If you’re a foodie, you’re in for a treat with a lineup that includes BakeHouse by KLCG, The Café by Royal Selangor, Courthouse Cafe, Kaw Kaw Malaya, and Gula Camca.

The new Kuala Lumpur City Gallery within the building chronicles three centuries of our evolution, and for a limited time, entry to these galleries is completely complimentary.

Looking ahead, the Warisan KL project will even include a pedestrian bridge by the end of this year, linking the historic Seri Negara and Carcosa directly to the Perdana Botanical Gardens.

Whether you are a local history buff or a curious traveller, this is your chance to reclaim a piece of the city that was "off-limits" for far too long.

Photo credit: The Star

For a peek at the stunning interiors and the latest "story-worthy" moments, follow them on Instagram at @bsas.kl or check out bsas.com.my.

It’s grand, it’s deeply significant, and it’s finally open for you to experience from within.

Photo credit: BSAS via Instagram

Quick Summary:

The Big News: Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad (Block 1) is officially open to the public as of 2 February 2026.

  • One Destination: The complex unifies 6 landmarks (including the Old Post Office and National Textile Museum) into a single cultural hub.
  • Limited Time Offer: Entry to the KL City Gallery is complimentary for the first month!
  • Eat & Explore: Home to BakeHouse by KLCG, Courthouse Cafe, and new craft-making spaces.
  • Visiting Info: Open daily from 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM at Dataran Merdeka.

Now that the doors are open, what part of the building’s 100-year history are you most curious to discover?

items.title: NIPAH VIRUS: WHAT’S HAPPENING AT THE BORDERS?
items.content:
Planning your next food hunt in KL or a weekend getaway to the highlands?

You might have seen some headlines floating around about the Nipah virus recently.

Since we’re your savvy local friend, we want to make sure you have the full picture; no panic, just the facts.

Photo credit: New York Post

So, what exactly is the deal with this virus?

In simple terms, Nipah is a virus that usually hangs out with fruit bats and can sometimes jump to other animals or people.

Interestingly, it was actually first discovered right here in Malaysia, specifically in Kampung Sungai Nipah, Negeri Sembilan, back in 1998.

Because of that history, Malaysia is widely considered one of the most prepared countries in the world when it comes to handling this.

Right now, our Health Minister, Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad, has confirmed that the Ministry of Health (MOH) is proactively monitoring reports from West Bengal, India.

But here is the part you’ll actually notice: "gatekeepers" at our borders are on high alert.

Whether you are arriving via KLIA Terminal 1 or 2, seaports, or land crossings, there is increased health surveillance and risk-based screening in place.

This doesn't mean travel is blocked or delayed; it just means our medical teams are being extra vigilant to keep everyone safe.

Think of it like a VIP security check: it’s a quiet, professional layer of protection ensuring your holiday remains uninterrupted.

The MOH has already put emergency medical teams on standby and established clear referral pathways just in case anyone feels under the weather.

As a traveller, your job is simple: practice basic hygiene, wash your hands often, and avoid raw date palm sap or fruit that looks like a bat might have had a snack first.

We’ll keep an eye on the situation for you, but for now, the city is wide open and ready for you to explore.

Stay smart, stay healthy, and enjoy VM2026!

Quick Summary:

  • Border Update: Expect upped health screenings at all international airports, seaports, and land borders.

  • The Situation: MOH is verifying reports from West Bengal, India, and has emergency teams on standby.

  • Local History: Since the virus was first found in Negeri Sembilan (1998), Malaysia’s response protocols are world-class.

  • Action Plan: No travel bans are in place; just keep up with basic hygiene and stay informed.

items.title: SKIP THE TRAFFIC: FREE TRAIN RIDES TO BATU CAVES FOR THAIPUSAM 2026!
items.content:
Getting to Batu Caves for Thaipusam just got a whole lot easier (and cheaper) this year.

Photo credit: CK Travels

In a move that’ll have your wallet doing a happy dance, KTMB has announced free train rides for the KTM Komuter Klang Valley sector.

This isn’t just for locals; whether you’re a traveller from abroad or a domestic tourist, you can ride the rails for RM0.00.

The free rides are valid for two full days, specifically from midnight on January 31 until 11:59 PM on February 1, 2026.

Photo credit: KTMB

If you’ve ever tried to drive toward Batu Caves during this festival, you’ll know that "traffic jam" is an understatement, it’s more like a permanent parking lot.

Taking the train is the ultimate pro-move to reach the world-famous golden Lord Murugan statue without the headache of finding a parking spot that doesn't exist.

Photo credit: The Star

The free travel covers the entire Klang Valley sector, including the Batu Caves–Pulau Sebang, Batu Caves–Port Klang, and Tanjung Malim–KL Sentral routes.

To make things even smoother, 28 stations will operate 24 hours a day for four days straight, from January 30 to February 2.

Photo credit: KTMB via Facebook

For those coming from further away, KTMB is also adding eight extra Electric Train Service (ETS) trips from January 29 to February 3.

These extra ETS trains cover high-demand routes like JB Sentral–Butterworth and KL Sentral–Ipoh, with over 10,000 extra tickets up for grabs.

Whether you're taking the Komuter or the ETS, you still need to tap your Touch ‘n Go card or use the KITS mobile app at the gantry.

Don't worry about the fare as for those two golden days, the amount deducted will be exactly zero.

Just make sure your card has the minimum balance (usually RM5) to trigger the gate, and you're good to go.

It’s the perfect excuse to experience the raw, spiritual energy of one of Malaysia’s most iconic cultural events without the logistical nightmare.

The Quick Summary:

  • What: Free KTM Komuter rides (Klang Valley sector) and 8 extra ETS trips.

  • When: Free rides on Jan 31 & Feb 1; 24-hour service from Jan 30 to Feb 2.

  • Where: Best for reaching Batu Caves; extra ETS for intercity travel.

  • How: Use Touch ‘n Go, KITS app, or debit/credit cards at the gates.

items.title: KL 2026 IS THE NEW 2016: WHY WE’RE TRADING BEIGE CAFES FOR UNFILTERED NIGHTS
items.content:
If you’ve been scrolling through TikTok lately, you’ve probably seen the viral "2026 is the new 2016" trend flooding your feed with grainy, high-flash photos and nostalgic throwbacks.

It turns out, the internet has collectively decided that ten years is enough time to miss the chaos of Puppy Dog filters and the simpler joy of the pre-AI era.

Photo credit: Free Malaysia Today

In Kuala Lumpur, this isn’t just a digital trend; it’s a full-blown lifestyle shift that is changing how we eat, play, and explore our concrete jungle.

We are officially entering our "Unfiltered Era," where the curated perfection of 2025 is being traded for the raw, vibrant energy of the mid-2010s.

Forget the quiet, minimalist cafes with white walls; 2026 is about the loud, neon-soaked nights and the thrill of a spontaneous squad photo.

Visit Malaysia 2026 is leaning hard into this vibe, focusing on "Surreal Experiences" that prioritise how a place feels over how it looks on a grid.

Think of it as a return to "Main Character Energy," where you aren’t just a spectator of the city, but the star of your own messy, colourful adventure.

Photo credit: Instagram @turbulency

The heart of this movement is a collective longing for authenticity in a world that has become a bit too "polished" and digitally distant.

We want to touch the textures of the city, taste the smoky heat of street food at Jalan Alor, and laugh until our faces hurt without worrying about the lighting.

Photo credit: Instagram @avostay_my

In 2016, we chased virtual monsters at the KLCC Park and Batu Caves during the Pokémon GO craze; in 2026, we are chasing that same sense of community and wonder.

Photo credit: Vulcan Post

Pro-Tip: When hunting for that 2016 vibe, ditch the DSLR and use your phone’s high-flash—the grainier and "vibier," the better.

You’ll notice the city’s newest hotspots, like the rooftop city park and bars in TRX, are trading "minimalist chic" for bold, spice-themed interiors and panoramic neon views.

Photo credit: Pentago Malaysia

Even our infrastructure is getting a glow-up, with permanent light installations in Bukit Bintang designed to make the streets feel like a 24/7 festival.

Photo credit: The Star

So, put down the editing apps, grab your most comfortable "athleisure" gear, and get ready to live like it’s 2016 all over again.

Because in Kuala Lumpur, the best memories aren't the ones that are perfectly edited, they’re the ones that are a little blurry and a lot of fun.

The Cultural Cheat Sheet

  • DO embrace the "High Flash" aesthetic for your night-out photos; it’s the unofficial uniform of the 2026 trend.

  • DO revisit the city’s "OG" landmarks like KLCC Park and Batu Caves; they are the heart of the nostalgia wave.

  • DON'T over-edit your experiences; the goal of 2026 is to capture the raw, "potato-quality" joy of the moment.

  • DON'T forget to lepak (hang out) without your phone for at least an hour. Ttrue 2016 energy is about being "presently" chaotic.

  • DO use local slang like Huat Ah! when celebrating a win; it’s timeless, high-energy, and always in style.

What was your favourite memory from 2016, and have you found that same "vibe" anywhere in KL lately?

items.content:
If you are passing through KLIA Terminal 1 recently, you might notice the air feels a little fresher and the vibe significantly more "healing."

To celebrate Visit Malaysia 2026 (VM2026), Malaysia Airports has officially unveiled Rimba, a nature-inspired transit sanctuary located right in the heart of the Satellite Building.

Photo credit: Utusan

Spanning a massive 970 square metres, this isn’t just a few potted plants. It is a complete reimagining of the former Jungle Boardwalk, designed to bring Malaysia’s rich natural character directly to you.

The space has been given a total "glow-up" with refreshed entrances and enriched greenery that create a stunning visual break from the usual airport hustle.

One of the coolest features is the locally crafted rattan lighting, which casts a warm, golden glow over the area, making it feel more like a boutique resort than a departure hall.

Everything here is built for relaxation, featuring garden-style seating specifically designed to help domestic and international travellers ease that inevitable travel fatigue.

Photo credit: Bernama

As MAHB Managing Director Dato' Mohd Izani Ghani puts it, Rimba offers a "comfortable pause" between flights, allowing the 50 to 60 million people passing through each year to truly feel Malaysia's hospitality.

It’s part of a much wider programme to uplift the KLIA experience, ensuring that whether you are just landing or waiting for a connection, you’re surrounded by our national identity.

For those of us travelling domestically, it’s a proud new landmark to show off to the world, and for our international guests, it’s the perfect first (or last) impression of our tropical home.

The best part? This is just Phase 1 and soon, we can expect even more premium retail offerings and upgraded connection desks to make your journey even smoother.

So, next time you have a layover, don’t just stay at your gate; follow the greenery to the Satellite Building and find your zen in the jungle.

It’s officially our year to shine, and Rimba is the perfect place to start your VM2026 adventure!

  • Location: KLIA Terminal 1, located in the Satellite Building (formerly the Jungle Boardwalk).

  • Pro Tip: Perfect for a "forest walk" selfie or a quiet spot to recharge your soul (and your phone!) between flights.

items.title: EVERYTHING YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT THE PONGGAL FESTIVAL
items.content:
If you’re wandering through Brickfields or Batu Caves today and hear joyful shouting alongside the smell of sweet, boiling milk, you’ve just found Ponggal!

Photo credit: Malay Mail

The Malaysia Hindu Sangam has officially confirmed that the peak of this vibrant harvest festival falls on January 15, 2026.

For travellers and locals alike, this is Malaysia’s version of "Thanksgiving": a four-day tribute to the Sun God, nature, and the cattle that help put food on our tables.

The name "Ponggal" literally means "to boil over," which is why the main ritual involves cooking a pot of rice, milk, and jaggery until it spills over the rim.

When that milk overflows, everyone shouts "Ponggalo Ponggal!" because a messy stove today means a life overflowing with prosperity and wealth all year round.

Photo credit: The Star

The festival is a four-day journey of renewal, starting with Bhogi on January 14, where homes are scrubbed clean and old belongings are discarded to make way for fresh starts.

Today, January 15, is the main event (Thai Ponggal), where families set up clay pots outdoors and decorate their entrances with stunning Kolams (intricate floor art made of colored rice).

But for those who just got hitched, this year is even more significant because of a tradition called Thala Ponggal.

Thala Ponggal is the very first harvest festival for a newly wedded couple, and it’s a massive milestone celebrated with the bride’s family.

The newlyweds are often gifted new clothes and brass utensils, and they work together to tend the fire, symbolising their first joint effort to bring "sweetness" into their new home.

The celebration continues on January 16 with Mattu Ponggal, a day dedicated entirely to honouring cattle.

You’ll see cows and bulls bathed, their horns painted in bright colours, and draped in garlands as a massive "thank you" for their hard work in the fields.

Finally, the festival wraps up on January 17 with Kaanum Ponggal, which is all about family reunions and younger generations seeking blessings from their elders.

If you’re a traveller in KL, head over to Little India in Brickfields. It’s the best place to soak in the atmosphere, snap photos of the sugarcane-lined streets, and try a bowl of that delicious sweet rice.

It’s a beautiful, noisy, and heartwarming celebration that perfectly captures the "Truly Asia" spirit of our city.

Quick Summary

  • The Main Event: January 15, 2026 is the official peak day for Thai Ponggal.

  • Thala Ponggal: A special "First Ponggal" for newlyweds, traditionally celebrated at the bride's family home.

  • The Ritual: Boiling sweet rice until it overflows to symbolise future abundance.

  • Four Days of Fun: Runs from January 14 (Cleaning) to January 17 (Family Reunions).

  • Best Spot for Tourists: Little India (Brickfields) or the Batu Caves temple grounds for the best visuals and festive food.

Have you ever tried the sweet "Ponggal" rice, or are you planning to visit Brickfields to see the colorful Kolams this weekend?

items.title: MCMC TO TAKE LEGAL ACTION AGAINST X AND XAI OVER USER SAFETY CONCERNS
items.content:
If you’ve noticed your X (formerly Twitter) feed acting a bit differently lately, there is a very big legal reason why.

Photo credit: Astro Awani

The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) has officially announced it is pursuing legal action against X Corp. and xAI LLC.

This move comes after the platform reportedly failed to address serious concerns regarding its AI chatbot, Grok, and its ability to generate harmful content.

Authorities have identified that the tool was being misused to create obscene, indecent, and non-consensual manipulated images, which is a massive no-go under Malaysian law.

The government is particularly worried about content involving women and minors, stating that the tech giants haven't done enough to stop it.

Even though Grok has already been temporarily restricted for users in Malaysia as of 11 January 2026, the MCMC is now taking things a step further in the courtroom.

While the X platform itself is still accessible, this legal battle marks a significant crackdown on how AI tools are regulated within our borders.

The Ministry of Communications has made it clear: if you want to operate in Malaysia, you have to follow the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998.

For the average traveller or local, this doesn't mean you need to stop tweeting your favourite laksa spots, but it does show that the digital landscape is tightening up.

Expect to see stricter moderation and perhaps more restrictions on AI features as the government pushes for a "safer internet" for everyone.

As always, keep your apps updated and stay mindful of what you're sharing in the digital sphere!

To know more about staying safe online, check out MCMC IG @skmm_mcmc

Quick Summary

  • The News: MCMC is suing X Corp. and xAI over safety failures.

  • The Reason: Misuse of the Grok AI to generate harmful and explicit images.

  • Current Status: Grok is currently restricted in Malaysia while legal proceedings begin.

  • The Law: Authorities are enforcing the Communications and Multimedia Act to protect users, especially women and children.

Do you think Malaysia is doing the right thing by taking social media giants to court over AI safety?

items.title: 5 SECRET SPOTS IN KL YOU PROBABLY HAVEN'T VISITED YET
items.content:
Photo credit: KL The Guide

Think you’ve seen all of KL? Think again! Whether you’re a local or just visiting, the city is packed with "hidden gems" that go way beyond the standard tourist trails.

From world-record pewter tanks to miniature worlds and deep dives into police history, here are five spots you need to add to your weekend itinerary.

Photo credit: KL The Guide

1. Royal Selangor Visitor Centre: Snap a Pic with a Giant!

Did you know the world’s largest pewter tankard is right here in KL? Standing tall at the Royal Selangor Visitor Centre, this iconic landmark is an absolute must-see.

While you're there, you can take a free guided tour to learn about Malaysia's rich tin mining history and watch master craftsmen at work. If you're feeling creative, sign up for a "School of Hard Knocks" workshop and hammer out your very own pewter masterpiece to take home!

  • 📍 Location: 4, Jalan Usahawan 6, Setapak Jaya, 53300 Kuala Lumpur.

  • ⏰ Hours: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM daily.

  • 🎟️ Admission: FREE entry.

Photo credit: KL The Guide

2. Bank Negara Malaysia Museum and Art Gallery: Cash, Coins, and Culture

If you ever wondered what early currency looked like or how our economy actually works, this is the place to be. Located at Sasana Kijang, this museum features six galleries, including a massive Numismatics (money) collection and a stunning Art Gallery showcasing Southeast Asian works.

The "Nautilus" staircase is also one of the most Instagrammable architectural spots in the city!

  • 📍 Location: Sasana Kijang, 2 Jalan Dato' Onn, 50480 Kuala Lumpur.

  • ⏰ Hours: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (Closed on Mondays).

  • 🎟️ Admission: FREE entry.

Photo credit: KL The Guide

3. MinNature Malaysia: Malaysia, But Way Smaller

Experience Malaysia from a completely different perspective—literally! MinNature Malaysia is Asia’s largest indoor miniature exhibition, featuring incredibly detailed 1:87 scale models of our most famous landmarks.

From the busy streets of Bukit Bintang to the heritage buildings of Ipoh, the detail is mind-blowing. Keep an eye out for the interactive elements where you can trigger lights and movement with the push of a button.

  • 📍 Location: JUMPA @ Sungei Wang Plaza, Level 1, Bukit Bintang.

  • ⏰ Hours: 10:00 AM – 10:00 PM daily.

  • 🎟️ Admission: RM30 (Adults), RM20-25 (Seniors/Youth).

Photo credit: KL The Guide

4. Royal Malaysian Police Museum: A Hero’s Journey

For a change of pace, head over to the Royal Malaysian Police Museum in the lush Lake Gardens area. This museum houses an impressive collection of police uniforms, weapons, and vehicles, including actual armored cars and a vintage police plane parked right outside!

It offers a fascinating look at the evolution of law enforcement in Malaysia, from the Melaka Sultanate era right up to the modern day.

  • 📍 Location: 5, Jalan Perdana, Tasik Perdana, 50480 Kuala Lumpur.

  • ⏰ Hours: 10:00 AM – 6:00 PM (Closed on Mondays).

  • 🎟️ Admission: FREE entry.

Photo credit: KL The Guide

5. Art House Gallery Museum of Ethnic Arts: A Hidden Attic of Treasures

Tucked away on the second floor of the Central Market Annexe, this gallery feels like stepping into "Aladdin’s Cave." It’s a specialised museum and antique shop that showcases an incredible range of native artwork, carvings, and textiles, particularly from the ethnic groups of Borneo.

If you're lucky, you'll catch the curator, Leonard, who can give you a personalised tour and share the fascinating stories behind each artifact.

  • 📍 Location: Lot 3.04 & 3.05, Level 2, Annexe Building, Central Market.

  • ⏰ Hours: 11:00 AM – 7:00 PM daily.

  • 🎟️ Admission: FREE (though some special tours may carry a small fee).

 Quick Summary

  • Best for Families: MinNature Malaysia and Royal Selangor Visitor Centre.

  • Best for History Buffs: Royal Malaysian Police Museum and Bank Negara Museum.

  • Best for Art Lovers: Art House Gallery and Bank Negara Art Gallery.

  • Budget Friendly: 4 out of 5 of these spots offer FREE admission!

Which of these hidden gems are you hitting up first this weekend? Let us know in the comments!

items.title: DON'T MISS OUT! AIRASIA X FINALLY BRINGS BACK EXCLUSIVE DIRECT FLIGHTS TO BUSAN
items.content:
Photo credit: visitbusan.net

If you’ve been dreaming of fresh seafood by the coast and colourful hillside villages, I have some fantastic news for your travel bucket list.

AirAsia X has officially announced its return to Busan, the stunning coastal sister to Seoul that we’ve all been missing.

Starting from 17 June 2026, you can hop on a direct flight from Kuala Lumpur (KUL) straight to Busan (PUS).

This isn’t just any comeback; it’s the only direct service connecting Malaysia to South Korea’s second-largest city.

Gone are the days of awkward layovers or long train rides from Seoul just to get your beach fix.

The airline is kicking things off with four weekly flights, making it perfect for a long weekend escape or a deep dive into Korean culture.

Photo credit: AirAsia X

For those who haven't been, Busan is like the cool, laid-back cousin of Seoul with better views and incredible vibes.

You can explore the "Santorini of the East" at Gamcheon Culture Village or take a sunset stroll along Haeundae Beach.

Photo credit: visitbusan.net

Now, let’s talk about the best part, the price.

To celebrate the relaunch, they are dropping promotional fares starting from just MYR 299 all-in one-way for economy!

If you’re feeling fancy, you can even snag a Premium Flatbed from MYR 999 for that extra comfort on the way to your seafood feast.

You’ll need to be quick, though, because these promo fares are only available from now until 18 January 2026.

Whether you're a KL-ite looking for a new adventure or a traveller using KL as your gateway, this route is a total game-changer for North Asia travel.

So, dust off your winter coat (or your breezy seaside outfits) because South Korea just got a whole lot closer.

To know more about the latest flight deals, check out AirAsia IG @flyairasia

Quick Summary

  • Route: Direct flights from Kuala Lumpur (KUL) to Busan (PUS).

  • Launch Date: Flights officially resume on 17 June 2026.

  • Frequency: 4 times weekly.

  • Booking: Promo fares from MYR 299 (Economy) or MYR 999 (Premium Flatbed).

  • Booking Deadline: Grab the deal before 18 January 2026.

  • Travel Period: Valid for trips between 17 June and 30 November 2026.

Don't miss out on these launch prices! Make sure to book your seats before the promo ends this Sunday.

items.title: MEET PANDA STARS CHEN XING AND XIAO YUE AT ZOO NEGARA!
items.content:
The wait is officially over, and things are about to get un-bear-ably cute at Zoo Negara.

After a month of staying behind the scenes to finish their mandatory quarantine, our two newest VIPs (Very Important Pandas) have finally made their public debut!

Photo credit: Media Selangor

Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim officially launched the Giant Panda Conservation Centre (GPCC) on Saturday, 10 January 2026, making it the hottest ticket in town right now.

The stars of the show are Chen Xing (the handsome male) and Xiao Yue (the lovely female), who arrived from China back in November.

But before you rush over with your camera, you should know that the zoo is playing it cool to keep the pandas stress-free.

To ensure Chen Xing and Xiao Yue don't get overwhelmed by their new fans, the zoo is limiting entry to only 50 visitors every five minutes.

That means you’ll get a solid five minutes of "aww-ing" time before the caretakers usher you out to let the next group in.

Think of it like a high-stakes speed date with a fluffy giant, where every second counts!

Now, here is some fantastic news for the parents looking to plan a family day out.

To celebrate the introduction of the panda pair and the upcoming festive season, Zoo Negara is offering a 50% discount on walk-in tickets for children (ages 3–12)!

You’ll have to be quick, though, because this special promo is only valid from 11 January until 24 January 2026.

It is the perfect excuse to gather the little ones and head over to Hulu Kelang for a budget-friendly adventure while the "panda-fever" is still fresh.

Just remember the golden rule: Chen Xing and Xiao Yue are sensitive to noise, so remind the kids to keep their voices low (even if the pandas are doing something adorable like eating bamboo).

Ready to see if they’re as fluffy in person as they are on the news?

The Need-to-Know

  • Location: Giant Panda Conservation Centre (GPCC), Zoo Negara, Ampang.
  • The Stars: Chen Xing (Male) and Xiao Yue (Female).
  • Opening Date: Now open to the public as of 10 January 2026.
  • Special Promo: 50% discount for walk-in child tickets (ages 3–12).
  • Promo Validity: Valid from 11 January – 24 January 2026 only.
  • Viewing Rules: Strictly 50 visitors per 5-minute session to keep the pandas calm.
  • Viewing Hours: Follows standard Zoo Negara hours (9:00 AM – 5:00 PM), but the Panda Centre usually closes around 4:30 PM.

To know more, check out Zoo Negara IG @zoonegara_malaysia
